Buddy_Line_1: Psst — no cookies, no analytics. You're off the grid here.
Buddy_Line_2: In the project preview page there is this site, inside this site. Inside this site.
Buddy_Line_3: Try the dark theme. The links pop harder.
Buddy_Line_4: Self-hosting AI is where my weekends go.
Buddy_Line_5: Every color on this page is tweakable. Gear icon, top right.
Buddy_Line_6: Built from scratch. No UI library was harmed.
Buddy_Line_7: From Ticino, working in the matrix.
Buddy_Line_8: Click me again. I have more to say.
Buddy_Line_9: "Genius doesn't exist — and sometimes it's even an idiot." [cit.]
Site_Description:
Needing a space to showcase my work, I made the site proof of what I could do. I opted to create as much as possible in an original way (such as the localization system or the possibility of changing themes) without using libraries precisely to be more flexible and effective in maintaining the code. 
I have, at times, re-adapted part of the code taken online, as it is also part of the work of an engineer. Working on it allowed me to strengthen my knowledge of the technologies used and learn how to use animations better.
 From time to time I come back to update and upgrade it.
OOEdit_Description: OO Edit is the internal tool I'm building with Oovie Studios to support their work. Oovie Studios produces AI-generated films synchronized to live music, where the visuals shift in real time with the performance; they've already shipped this kind of experience with brands like Dell Technologies and Luxottica, and with cultural venues like Teatro Verdi and La Filarmonica. The tool lets the team assemble storyboards for these productions and use AI to modify clips inside the same workspace, so the full loop from idea to final shot stays in one place. I'm on both the frontend (React) and the backend (Node.js + MongoDB). Ongoing.
LibraryManager_Description:
Library Manager is a personal project I built to keep track of my comic collection and the rest of my library. I wanted a single place to hold every detail of every volume: author, cost, condition, and anything else worth recording. On top of that, I measured every shelf at home and gave each manga its own depth, so the tool runs an algorithm that, under my own placement rules, decides the best position for every book across the shelves.

It runs on an Arduino-based server on my home network, with a React frontend, a Node.js backend and SQLite for storage. Since the system knows where each volume lives, I also keep a small tablet next to the shelves to look things up, the same way a public-library catalog terminal works. Built solo in about three weeks in March 2026. Done.
Minerva_Description:
Minerva is my Intelligent Neural Enhanced Responsive Virtual Assistant. She's a fully local, self-hosted system I've been building solo, in my free time, as my personal AI assistant.

Under the hood she can run different AI models for image generation, large language models, voice recognition and transcription, music diarization, and text-to-speech. A RAG layer gives her persistent memory across sessions, and she has different operating modes, from normal conversation to code writing, each with its own behavior.

Everything runs on hardware in my home, which means she's genuinely slow: local inference needs a lot of power, and I'm still scaling the rig up as I go. This is deliberately a long-term project. I keep updating her, optimizing what's there, and adding more hardware muscle. Next on the list is visual recognition, plus mail and PC scanning so she can help me sort files and emails automatically.

The stack is wide: React, Node.js, Python, FastAPI and Docker are the spine. It's also where most of what I've learned over the years in IT is quietly pouring in.
LastRun_Description:
During the Game Development course at Supsi (Italian Swiss University School), I developed, together with a course colleague, the creation of a game of my choice, inspired by Fall Guys, created for a single player.
 We have also developed everything needed for a primary game. I worked particularly hard on the procedural generation of game paths. This project was helpful in strengthening the knowledge, then still only amateur, regarding Unity.
TTC_Description:
In conjunction with my experience in G3CLabs, I had the opportunity to experiment with a working form in react.
 After three days in which I only partially dedicated myself to the project, I still obtained an appreciated result, even though I wasn't entirely satisfied with the graphics. In particular, the graphics relating to dynamic flowers and leaves on the input board were appreciated.
 Within the project, I used different code styles (class components | functional components).
SmashWorld_Description:
Before starting to work on my thesis, I was hired at G3CLabs (Rome) as a developer.
 The work was focused on creating a platform where users could have a 3D experience within a metaverse and had to be able to support VR and an application for Android and IOS and cross-platforming. The project was developed entirely from scratch, allowing me to be involved in creating the entire system architecture.
 In two years, I saw a large project born and grow, working daily on both the client and server sides. This is the work that most allowed me to learn the use of Unity and C# and refine my software architecture skills. I also had the chance to work on distributed systems and develop a working multiplayer system.
LudoGame_Description:
This project was carried out together with a course colleague; we learned how to use Photonengine, the central multiplayer API system for Unity.
 To do this, we developed the virtual version of the LUDO game and are also thinking about expanding it. Each pawn in the game is equivalent to a character with its specific abilities. The development of this project was fundamental to fully understanding the different challenges of an online gaming system and how to solve them effectively.
Tesi_Description:
I developed my dissertation in the field of Game Development. The goal was to create, in Unity, a procedurally generated world similar to that of Minecraft. 
I used an Octree, a tree structure, to represent the world. The Octree's peculiarity is the leaves' variable size, intentionally used to improve gaming performance. Thanks to this, I opted to generate several chunks on the Y axis of the world, unlike Minecraft, where this does not happen. Another fundamental aspect of the work was the attention to the extensibility and optimization of the code. 
The thesis was evaluated very positively, as I provided an immediately fungible product.
privacy_summary: This site does not use tracking cookies, analytics, or third-party trackers. Your preferences (language, theme) are stored only in your browser.
privacy:
Last updated: 16 April 2026

This website does not use tracking cookies, analytics, advertising, or third-party trackers.

1. Data collected
None. I do not collect, store, or process any personal data on servers I control.

2. Browser storage
The site uses your browser's localStorage to remember your display preferences (language, theme, animations, and whether this notice has been dismissed). This data never leaves your device and is only read by the site running in your browser. You can clear it any time via your browser settings.

3. Embedded content
Some pages may embed content from third parties (e.g. YouTube, Vimeo) when you interact with that content. Those providers may set their own cookies; please refer to their own privacy policies.

4. Your rights (GDPR)
Since I process no personal data, there is no personal data to access, correct, delete, or export. If you have questions, you can contact me directly.

5. Changes
Any updates to this notice will be posted on this page with a new revision date.

6. Contact
Diego Vagni — diegovagni.dv@gmail.com — www.diegovagni.com
Comunication_Framework_Description: In March 2023, having worked with Photonengine and having noticed its limitations, I decided to try to build a library independently. The project's objective is to develop a system that is easy to integrate within any C# application that allows communication via the network. The library already offers excellent support for creating centralized applications and software. The library defines everything necessary to operate a server and a client, adding a user and resource control system. The system is highly expandable and includes all the capabilities gained over the year. The git will be available as soon as the documentation is finished, and I can make it open source.
TwitchStats_Description: During the Data Science course at SUPSI, I developed a group project in Python, using libraries such as Pandas and NumPy. The dataset was provided by a colleague who used data extracted from his Twitch account. This project was fundamental to reinforce the notions learned during the course and the use of Python.
FirstSteps_Description:
I was born in Milan on February 1, 1994. The passion for IT didn't come from any single moment, it just clicked from within, early.

I took the scientific high school route and graduated in 2012. Since I wasn't coming from a technical institute, the university I was heading toward required a preparatory internship, which is how I ended up at H-Farm, a startup incubator, for a few months. I spent that time inside two of its startups, Zoopa and Fungosgames, mostly studying and absorbing how work actually happens in a young company: the pace, the trade-offs, the improvisation.

That experience also took me to London for several months, where I worked as a beta tester and studied at an international school, sharpening both the technical instinct and the English.

In parallel, since I was young I spent a few months a year at a meditation school in the Marche region. Practicing different techniques there ended up shaping how I work more than I expected, especially on focus and on getting along with a team.
H-Farm_Description:
H-Farm is a startup incubator in Treviso. My few months there were split across two of its startups, Zoopa and Fungosgames, doing a mix of prototyping and bugfixing. Front-end was simply where they placed me, the easiest starting point at that age, and since React had just put out its first release, I ended up picking it up from the very beginning.

At Zoopa most of my work was mobile-responsiveness checks: running the UI across different devices to see how it rendered. Fungosgames was more open-ended experimentation. It was a useful first look at how things actually move inside a small company: fast, informal, with a lot of self-direction.
TAG_Description:
I had to freeze university for a year, so I looked at the curriculum of the year I'd be skipping and picked a direction that would be easy to catch up on later. Web development, mostly React, was the obvious one: I'd already been using it since H-Farm, and a certified course somewhere would leave me with a paper covering the same ground. TAG Innovation School in Milan was what I landed on.

Three months full-time, with a five-day stop at TAG's Barcelona campus halfway through to work there as well. The capstone was an Airbnb-like front-end, built in a team.

Toward the end, TAG plugged us into a one-day hackathon Vodafone was running, looking for talent for their chatbot TOBI. Several teams, one day on the clock. My team built a very small prototype on top of the tools Vodafone handed us, nothing fancy, and it won. That's what opened the door to the next step.
Vodafone_Description:
From July 2018 I spent nine months inside Vodafone's TOBI tribe. TOBI is the company's omnichannel chatbot, operating in parallel on the website, the iOS and Android apps, and handling phone calls before handing off to a human operator if needed. Day to day I was reading through real customer conversations, flagging where the bot was failing and where the flows could be tightened, and writing new dialogue branches directly inside their proprietary platform.

It was my first taste of a really large company, and honestly it confirmed I preferred the pace of startups: the rhythm was a bit colder, more layered. When the internship ended I went back to SUPSI to continue the degree.

Looking at it now, that was an early stage of both omnichannel chatbots and voice recognition systems. Given where language models and conversational AI have ended up since, having my hands on those pieces back then feels even more valuable in hindsight than it did at the time.
PartTime_Description:
There's been a part-time job running in the background of everything else on this page, quietly, for more than a decade. It started with Loram SA around the same time I began university: light system management work, nothing heavy, but a steady ground-line that kept me inside real production systems while everything else (uni, TAG, Vodafone) rotated around it. I stayed there until 2018.

Since 2018 I've been part-time at Alamar GmbH, based in Zug and fully remote. Alamar is a consultancy, which is the setup that actually suits me best: they place me inside startups as needed, so the work keeps shifting, systems to manage, things to troubleshoot, and, increasingly, questions about whether and how a new technology is worth folding into a client's processes. It's exactly the kind of variety and pace that big-company work couldn't give me, and it's why I'm still there.
SUPSIME_Description:
I moved to Switzerland with my family at 16 and finished high school there, so staying in the country for university felt natural: SUPSI in Manno was close and a good fit. In late 2014 I started a Computer Engineering degree there.

SUPSI's methodology is lab-heavy, which meant years of actual projects, solo and in groups, touching a wide spread of IT fields. That's where the base I use today was built, and picking up the languages and tools of the moment came with the territory. The pace made programming languages start to feel really similar under the hood, so switching between them and learning a new one quickly became second nature. It was also a solid way to get used to figuring things out on my own. My favorite courses were object-oriented programming and game development; the gamedev project is LastRun, over on the projects page.

In 2018 I took a year out for a web development course at the TAG Innovation School in Milan, which also got me into a Vodafone hackathon on TOBI, their automated customer-support system. My team won, and that opened a multi-month internship at the Milan office. I came back to SUPSI in 2019 and delivered my thesis in June 2021, wrapping up the degree. The thesis also has its own entry on the projects page.
G3CLABSME_Description:
G3CLabs was a Rome-based startup, and my first placement through Alamar's consultancy model. I came on as a fullstack developer in March 2021, right before the thesis submission, at a point where I was the only one actually coding. Over the two years that followed I built out the application, and as the team grew around me I ended up being the person who knew the codebase, which naturally pushed me into mentoring newcomers and carrying a lot of the technical decisions.

The product was a multi-user 3D environment, spatial audio, text chat, iOS and Android, built in Unity with C#, and the work covered the whole stack, from architecture out to production. The networking layer was a major part of the job, and that's where I put down a solid foundation in multiplayer development. I kept automated tests in the loop and shipped to prod continuously. It's the project SmashWorld on the projects page; the deeper technical details live there.

My role ended in early 2023, not because anything went wrong but because the contract had delivered what it asked to deliver, a clean exit by design.
OnlyMe_Description:
In March 2023, once the G3CLabs contract ended, I took a long sabbatical, not because I needed a break so much as because I wanted to stop and see what was actually happening in the technology landscape, at my own pace. It ran until summer 2025. The part-time at Alamar kept running through all of it, just without taking on new consultancy placements; I stayed inside the business handling its IT.

A good chunk of that time went into a networking library I built for myself, to make starting future projects smoother; it lives on the projects page as the Communication Framework. In parallel I worked on Character Builder, a study of how to modularize characters for videogames, also on the projects page.

The rest of it was AI. I followed what OpenAI and the open-source ecosystem were doing closely, took a couple of Udemy courses on AI, LLMs and prompting to fill in the more structured gaps, and spent real time with AUTOMATIC1111 running Stable Diffusion locally. That period is where the AI skills on the skills page, prompting, self-hosting, image and video generation, first took root. Everything here was a study project; for the technical details, the projects page is the right entry point.
BackToConsulence_Description:
By summer 2025 I was back in consultancy mode, both placements still via Alamar.

The first was Fongit, the startup incubator of the canton of Geneva. I shipped an internal tool for rating and selecting which startups to back, with an LLM doing the information parsing and the initial ranking. It was solo work, a couple of months end to end; the deeper details live on its project card on the projects page.

In January 2026 I joined Oovie Studios, a studio working on live music paired with generated video. I'm part of the team on one of their internal tools, covering both the frontend and backend sides. Still ongoing.

The sabbatical year also left me with a different kind of creativity, one that makes personal projects start on their own without needing a contract to justify them. A few of those (library manager, Minerva, others) live on the projects page.
AIRapport_Description:
Since AI has become part of everyday work, my question has mostly been how to live with it, not whether to use it. The honest answer is that vibe coding, built on top of the years of experience I already had, pushed my work a clear step forward. It's faster, more precise, and leaves me with enough headroom to run several projects in parallel without things falling apart.

The effect I didn't expect was on creativity. Having an AI around to offload the plumbing means more of my time goes to ideas that used to stay in my head. Minerva, on the projects page, is one of those: by its nature it couldn't exist without AI.
Passions_Description:
Outside IT, I collect comics from all over the world and every era. The library has crossed 2,700 volumes, which makes picking a favorite impossible: too many, too different. Videogames are another constant, mostly simulation and sandbox, like Minecraft and Kerbal Space Program, which neatly overlaps with a broader love for science (and a weakness for random funny facts, especially about nature, space, and history).

Travel is the other big thread: mostly around Europe, but also stretching much farther when I get the chance. The remote-work setup with Alamar makes that a lot easier than it sounds.

YouTube usually runs in the background while I'm working on lighter tasks: scientific channels, cinema podcasts, TED talks, and pretty much anything about technology. It's how I keep up without having to actively carve out the time.

More recent additions: in 2025 I discovered Paul Shapera's music and his operas, which quickly became a regular listen. And since I was young, meditation has stayed with me, still part of the week.
